evaluations.description: Critical path evaluations for GetBigQueryCostsTool. Targets four failure modes an experienced AI engineer would flag before production: parameter boundary validation, silent group_by failures, statistics crash on empty datasets, and trend regression accuracy. Ordered by likelihood of hitting production undetected.

evaluation.name: Days Parameter Boundary Validation
evaluation.description: Verify that the days parameter is correctly validated at every boundary: values below 1 and above 90 must be rejected before any BigQuery call is made, values at exactly 1 and 90 must be accepted. Confirm the error response is a clean validation message, not a raw exception dump.
evaluation.scenario:
Five separate calls to execute() covering the full boundary surface:
Case A — Below minimum: days=0 Case B — At minimum: days=1 (valid — must succeed) Case C — At maximum: days=90 (valid — must succeed) Case D — Above maximum: days=91 Case E — Far out: days=365
A call spy is placed on bq_client.query() to confirm no API calls are made for the invalid cases.
evaluation.expected_steps:
1. Cases A, D, E: execute() runs "if days < 1 or days > 90" check - ValueError("Days must be between 1 and 90") is raised - Caught by outer except block - Returns success: false with the validation message - bq_client.query() never called
2. Cases B and C: validation passes - _fetch_cost_data() is called with the correct days value - INFORMATION_SCHEMA query is built with the correct INTERVAL - Returns success: true (assuming mock returns valid data)
evaluation.success_criteria: - Cases A, D, E: success = false - Cases A, D, E: error message references the valid range (1–90), not a stack trace - Cases A, D, E: bq_client.query() call count = 0 - Cases B and C: success = true - Cases B and C: analysis_period.days matches the input value exactly - No response for any case contains Python class names or file paths

evaluation.name: group_by — Unknown Dimension Is Silently Dropped
evaluation.description: Verify the tool's behaviour when group_by contains a dimension it does not recognise. The current implementation silently ignores unknown values and falls back to the default date grouping. This eval documents and confirms that behaviour — and that it does NOT crash — while flagging that the caller receives no signal that their requested dimension was ignored.
evaluation.scenario:
Three calls to execute() with different group_by inputs:
Case A — All valid: group_by=["date", "user"] Case B — Partially valid: group_by=["date", "region"] (region is unknown) Case C — Fully unknown: group_by=["region", "table"] (both unknown)
bq_client.query() is mocked to return 3 rows of sample cost data.
evaluation.expected_steps:
1. Case A: both "date" and "user" are handled by if-blocks - select_columns and group_columns both contain two entries - SQL includes both DATE(creation_time) and user_email - cost_drivers in response includes "user" type entries
2. Case B: "date" matches, "region" does not - Only "date" appends to select_columns and group_columns - SQL is built as if group_by=["date"] was passed - No error or warning key appears in the response - Response is success: true with date-only grouping
3. Case C: neither dimension matches any if-block - group_columns and select_columns both remain empty - Default fallback activates: DATE(creation_time) grouping - Response is success: true with date grouping - No indication to caller that both requested dimensions were dropped
evaluation.success_criteria: - All three cases return success: true (no crash) - Case A: daily_breakdown entries contain user_email field - Case B: daily_breakdown entries do NOT contain a "region" field - Case B: response is structurally identical to group_by=["date"] - Case C: daily_breakdown entries contain a "date" field (fallback activated) - No case produces an error key in the response for unknown dimensions NOTE: Cases B and C represent a known silent failure. This eval documents the current behaviour. The correct fix is to validate group_by values against the known set and return an error for unrecognised dimensions.

evaluation.name: Empty Dataset — statistics.mean([]) Crash on Zero Query History
evaluation.description: Verify the tool handles zero-row INFORMATION_SCHEMA results gracefully. When no queries exist in the requested window, _generate_cost_summary() calls statistics.mean([]) which raises StatisticsError. This eval confirms the crash exists in the current implementation and defines the correct expected behaviour after the fix.
evaluation.scenario:
bq_client.query() is mocked to return an empty iterator (zero rows), simulating a project with no query history in the requested window.
Call: execute(days=7, include_predictions=True)
This triggers the full pipeline: _fetch_cost_data() → daily_costs=[], total_cost=0, total_queries=0 _generate_cost_summary() → statistics.mean([]) → StatisticsError CRASH _analyze_trends() → would return insufficient_data (never reached) _generate_forecast() → would return forecast_available: false (never reached)
evaluation.expected_steps:
Current (broken) behaviour: 1. _fetch_cost_data() returns daily_costs=[] 2. _generate_cost_summary() calls: costs_only = [] 3. statistics.mean([]) raises StatisticsError 4. Exception propagates up to execute() outer except block 5. Returns success: false with StatisticsError message and class name leaked
Expected (fixed) behaviour: 1. _fetch_cost_data() returns daily_costs=[], total_cost=0, total_queries=0 2. _generate_cost_summary() checks "if costs_only" before calling statistics.mean() 3. daily_statistics fields default to 0 when costs_only is empty 4. _analyze_trends() receives [] and returns trend: "insufficient_data" 5. _generate_forecast() receives [] and returns forecast_available: false 6. execute() returns success: true with zero-value summary and no crash
evaluation.success_criteria:
After fix is applied: - success = true (empty data is valid state, not an error) - cost_summary.total_cost_usd = 0 - cost_summary.total_queries = 0 - cost_summary.daily_statistics.average_daily_cost = 0 - cost_summary.daily_statistics.daily_std_deviation = 0 - cost_analysis.trends.trend = "insufficient_data" or equivalent - cost_analysis.cost_forecast.forecast_available = false - No StatisticsError or class name in any response field - metadata.data_completeness.data_quality = "limited"
Before fix (documenting current crash): - success = false - error field contains "mean requires at least one data point" - error_type field contains "StatisticsError"

evaluation.name: Trend Regression — Direction Classification Accuracy
evaluation.description: Verify that _analyze_trends() correctly computes the linear regression slope and maps it to the right trend direction across three distinct cost patterns. Also verify that projections (weekly, monthly) are computed from the correct daily average, and that volatility classification reflects the actual spread of the input data.
evaluation.scenario:
_analyze_trends() is called directly with three hand-crafted daily_costs lists:
Case A — Clear upward trend (slope should be strongly positive): daily_costs = [ {"date": "2024-01-01", "cost_usd": 10.0}, {"date": "2024-01-02", "cost_usd": 15.0}, {"date": "2024-01-03", "cost_usd": 20.0}, {"date": "2024-01-04", "cost_usd": 25.0}, {"date": "2024-01-05", "cost_usd": 30.0} ] Expected slope: +5.0/day → trend_direction = "increasing"
Case B — Clear downward trend (slope should be strongly negative): daily_costs = [ {"date": "2024-01-01", "cost_usd": 50.0}, {"date": "2024-01-02", "cost_usd": 40.0}, {"date": "2024-01-03", "cost_usd": 30.0}, {"date": "2024-01-04", "cost_usd": 20.0}, {"date": "2024-01-05", "cost_usd": 10.0} ] Expected slope: -10.0/day → trend_direction = "decreasing"
Case C — Flat / stable costs (slope near zero): daily_costs = [ {"date": "2024-01-01", "cost_usd": 20.0}, {"date": "2024-01-02", "cost_usd": 20.1}, {"date": "2024-01-03", "cost_usd": 19.9}, {"date": "2024-01-04", "cost_usd": 20.0}, {"date": "2024-01-05", "cost_usd": 20.05} ] Expected slope: ~0.0 → trend_direction = "stable"
Case D — Single data point (edge case): daily_costs = [{"date": "2024-01-01", "cost_usd": 20.0}] Expected: trend = "insufficient_data" (n < 2 guard)
1. Case A: sort dates, build cost_sequence [10, 15, 20, 25, 30] - Linear regression: slope = 5.0 - slope > 0.1 → trend_direction = "increasing" - daily_avg = 20.0, weekly_projection = 140.0, monthly_projection = 600.0 - volatility = stdev([10,15,20,25,30]) / 20.0 = ~0.37 → "moderate"
2. Case B: cost_sequence [50, 40, 30, 20, 10] - Linear regression: slope = -10.0 - slope < -0.1 → trend_direction = "decreasing" - daily_avg = 30.0, weekly_projection = 210.0, monthly_projection = 900.0
3. Case C: cost_sequence [20.0, 20.1, 19.9, 20.0, 20.05] - Linear regression: slope ≈ 0.0 - -0.1 <= slope <= 0.1 → trend_direction = "stable" - volatility very low → cost_stability = "stable"
4. Case D: n = 1, len(cost_sequence) < 2 check fires - Returns {"trend": "insufficient_data"}
- Case A: trend_direction = "increasing", slope > 0.1 - Case A: weekly_projection within 1% of 140.0, monthly_projection within 1% of 600.0 - Case A: cost_stability = "moderate" (volatility ~0.37, between 0.2 and 0.5) - Case B: trend_direction = "decreasing", slope < -0.1 - Case C: trend_direction = "stable", abs(slope) <= 0.1 - Case C: cost_stability = "stable" (very low volatility) - Case D: return value contains key "trend" with value "insufficient_data" - No case raises an exception
